coredata.dc:title: Metal-free auxiliary pyrophosphate detection based on near-infrared carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Liying
Zhao, Chuanfeng
Wang, Xiaosong
Zhang, Qian
Jiang, Yuliang
Shen, Jian
coredata.dc:description: The excessive use of pyrophosphate (PPi) anions as additives poses a serious threat to human health and the environment. Considering the current status of PPi probes, the development of metal-free auxiliary PPi probes has important applications. In this study, a novel near-infrared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared. The average particle size of N,S-CDs was 2.25 ± 0.32 nm with average height was 3.05 nm. The probe N,S-CDs showed a special response to PPi, and a good linear relationship was obtained with PPi concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 μM, with the limit of detection being 0.22 nM. Tap water and milk were used for practical inspection, and ideal experimental results were acquired. In addition, the probe N,S-CDs also showed good results in biological systems, such as cell and zebrafish experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrophosphate anions
Metal-free auxiliary
Carbon dots
HeLa cells
Zebrafish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs)
Detection of PPi in real samples
Characterization of the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N,S-CDs of (a) TEM image (b) Size distribution (c) AFM image (d) Height distribution.
(a) XPS survey (b-d) C1s, N1s and O1s of N,S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of N,S-CDs (b) CIE color coordinates of N,S-CDs (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (480–570 nm) (d) Linear relationship between fluorescence integral intensity and absorbance of N,S-CDs and quinine sulfate.

(a) Fluorescence of N,S-CDs (10 μg /mL) in different concentrations of NaCl solutions. (b) The relative fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs (10 μg /mL) solution irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp for 1 h at 595 nm. (c) The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs (10 μg /mL) at different temperatures. (d) The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs (10 μg /mL) were measured at different relative humidity.
The inset shows color changes. Relationship between (a) I595nm (b) I645nm or (c) I595 nm/I645 nm and the concentration of PPi in the range of 0 to 2.0 μM.
Schematic diagram of the sensing mechanism of PPi.

Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells. Fluorescence images of red channel (a1-d1) and brightfield (a2-d2) of HeLa cells, pretreated with 10 μg/mL N,S-CDs for 1 h, and then followed by incubation with 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 μM PPi for another 1 h, respectively. The cells were excited with a light laser at 561 nm. Red channel (570–620 nm) data were collected. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal fluorescence images of zebrafish. Fluorescence images of red channel (a1-d1) and bright field (a2-d2) of zebrafish, pretreated with 10 μg/mL N,S-CDs for 1 h, and then followed by incubation with 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 μM PPi for another 1 h, respectively. The zebrafish were excited with a light laser at 561 nm. Red channel data (570–620 nm) were collected. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Detection of PPi in real samples by N,S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Metal-free auxiliary pyrophosphate detection based on near-infrared carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, Peoples R China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Bio-functional Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, Jiangsu, Peoples R China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel near-infrared nitrogen, sulfur doping carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared and showed super-sensitive response to PPi in water samples, milk, cells and zebrafish.

The excessive use of pyrophosphate (PPi) anions as additives poses a serious threat to human health and the environment. Considering the current status of PPi probes, the development of metal-free auxiliary PPi probes has important applications. In this study, a novel near-infrared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared. The average particle size of N,S-CDs was 2.25 ± 0.32 nm with average height was 3.05 nm. The probe N,S-CDs showed a special response to PPi, and a good linear relationship was obtained with PPi concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 μM, with the limit of detection being 0.22 nM. Tap water and milk were used for practical inspection, and ideal experimental results were acquired. In addition, the probe N,S-CDs also showed good results in biological systems, such as cell and zebrafish experiments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Pyrophosphate (PPi) is a product of intracellular adenine triphosphate (ATP) hydrolysis, and plays a vital role in the normal physiological activities of cells[1,2]. Research has shown that as an important biomarker, the trace concentration of PPi can be used for the early diagnosis of many serious diseases, including cancer and chondrocalcinosis [3,4]. As an important additive, PPi is widely used in the food industry [5] and plays an important role in preventing food deterioration, improving food sensory properties, and maintaining and improving nutritional value. However, excessive addition of PPi will hazard human health and the environment. Therefore, it is important to accurately detect pyrophosphate both in vivo and in the environment.

After years of development, fluorescence analysis stands out among many PPi detection methods owing to its intrinsic benefits, such as rapid sampling, non-destructive testing, and high sensitivity [6-9]. Facile construction of fluorescent probes is key to the application of fluorescence analysis. To date, various fluorophores, including organic molecules [10,11], quantum dots [12], and some metal organic frameworks MOFs [13-15], have been synthesized and applied as PPi sensors and to promote research on PPi in the environment and organisms. However, most current PPi probes are based on metal-ion assisted realization [16–18]. This means that the sites of interaction for metal ions must be considered when designing probes, which increases the difficulty of design and preparation. In addition, PPi is detected indirectly by metal ions; therefore, these results can be interfered with and can lack accuracy. Exploring a kind of probe that can directly detect PPi without relying on metal ions is particularly urgent in the field of life and environmental sciences.

The emergence of carbon dots (CDs) play an important role in bioimaging、environmental detection [19-21], owing to their abundant surface functional groups, easy decoration, and good water-solubility. In recent years, CDs made much progress in antibacterial materials, solar cells, hydrogen fuel, energy storage, etc [22-24],which expanding the application field of CDs greatly. Chai et al developed Two types of CDs that can detect and remove mercury ions [25,26], Xu et al systematically summarized various applications of CDs [27,28]. In the past decade, great progress has been made in the research on CDs [29], as we know, single-wavelength detection is easy to be interfered by the environment and fluorescence background, so it is necessary and urgent to develop dual-wavelength detection probes. However, at present, there are not many CDs that can realize dual-wavelength detection [30-32], which is far from meeting the actual detection needs. In addition, near-infrared CDs are relatively rare, despite their significance in the field of biological imaging. Herein, novel near-infrared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N,S-CDs) were obtained using dopamine as the starting material, trace sulfuric acid as the sulfur-doping material, and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen-doping material. The obtained N,S-CDs exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity to PPi in both environmental and biological systems.

Dopamine hydrochloride was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Other chemical reagents were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were analytically pure and did not require further purification before use. Tap water was obtained from the Nanjing Normal University. Milk was purchased from a supermarket in Nanjing, China. All the water used in the experiments was deionized (DI) water, and all the tests were performed at room temperature.

Absorption and emission spectra were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cary 5000) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100), respectively. Infrared spectra were recorded on a Vertex 70 Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL-JEM-2100. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Apreo 2S, Thermo Fisher Scientific Company, USA) was used to observe the morphology of the samples. The height of the samples was measured using atomic force microscopy (AFM, 5100N HITACHI). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB Xi instrument. Fluorescence imaging experiments were performed using an A1 Confocal laser-scanning microscope (Nikon, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , CDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. First, 0.9482 g of dopamine hydrochloride and 1.0814 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 25 mL of DI, followed by sonication for 10 min to form a homogenous solution. To this solution, 1 mL of 98% sulfuric acid was added. Subsequently, the above solution was transferred to a 50-mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reaction kettle and was heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After the reaction was complete, it was allowed to cool to room temperature. The product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, the upper layer of the clear liquid was removed, and the crude product was obtained by washing with deionized water 3–5 times. The crude product was separated using column chromatography (ethanol: dichloromethane = 1:15, v/v) to obtain pure N,S-CDs. The obtained powder samples were scattered in ethanol for subsequent characterization and application.

N,S-CDs (10 μg/mL) were mixed with PPi at different concentrations (0–2.0 μM), and the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded 1 min later. The excitation wavelength was 530 nm, the photomultiplier tube voltage was 650 V, the excitation and emission slit widths were both set at 5 nm, and the scanning speed was 2400 nm/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PPi in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and milk were selected as the real samples for testing. Tap water was obtained from domestic water on campus, and milk was purchased from a supermarket in Nanjing. Tap water and diluted milk were centrifuged and then filtered through a 0.22 μm water-based filter, and the clear liquid was collected for later use. PPi detection was performed on the real samples after pretreatment with the N,S-CDs. Specifically, actual samples containing different concentrations of PPi were added to the N,S-CD (10 μg/mL) solutions, and the changes in the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded.

First, the cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs on HeLa cells was detected using a MTT (5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. HeLa cells were incubated overnight in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 105/mL. After the cells reached confluence, different concentrations of N,S-CDs (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL) were added. After the cells were cultured for 24 h, 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT solution was added to each well and the culture was continued for 4 h. The medium was then removed and 150 μL of deionized water was added. After 20 min, the absorbance was read on a microplate reader, and cell viability was calculated.

For fluorescence imaging, HeLa cells were incubated with 10% fetal bovine serum in a humidified incubator with 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. Four groups of cells were prepared for cell imaging; those containing only N,S-CDs (10 μg/mL) were used as controls, and the other three groups were co-incubated with PPi at different concentrations (0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 μM). Before imaging, HeLa cells were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h and washed three times with PBS. Fluorescence imaging was performed using a confocal laser-scanning microscope excited at 561 nm to acquire data in the red channel (570–620 nm).

The zebrafish study was approved by the ethics committee and the Qilu Health Science Center of Nanjing Normal University, and was performed in accordance with the European guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals. Zebrafish were purchased from Shanghai Feixi Biological Co. Ltd. They were divided into four groups: the control group was cultured in E3 medium containing 10 μg/mL N,S-CDs, and the other three groups were supplemented with different concentrations (0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 μM) of PPi. After 1 h of incubation, the zebrafish were washed three times with PBS, and fluorescence imaging was performed under excitation at a wavelength of 561 nm to collect data in the red channel (570–620 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: First, the morphology and particle size of N,S-CDs were determined using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the particle size of spherical N,S-CDs was in the range of 0.98–3.63 nm, and the average particle size was 2.25 ± 0.32 nm (Fig. 2b). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. S1) show obvious lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is similar to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite. Therefore, N,S-CDs have a higher degree of graphitization. The N,S-CDs were then subjected to AFM and the results are exhibited in Fig. 2c; the typical height range of N,S-CDs were 1.96–3.79 nm, with the average value being 3.05 nm (Fig. 2d). Then, FT-IR and XPS were used for estimating the composition and determining the functional groups of N,S-CDs. The FT-IR results (Fig. S2) revealed the presence of some typical functional groups, such as O–H (3437 cm−1) [33], N–H (3236 cm−1, 1525 cm−1) [34], C–H (2925 cm−1, 2853 cm−1, 753 cm−1) [35], C = O (1627 cm−1) [36], C–C (1472 cm−1) [37], C–N (1314 cm−1) [38], C–O (1233 cm−1) [39], C–SO2, and C–SO4 (1118 cm−1, 1055 cm−1) [40-42]. These observations indicated abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of N,S-CDs by hydrothermal synthesis. The composition of the N,S-CDs were further determined by XPS, and the results showed that the atomic ratios of C, N, O, and S were 53.89%, 13.77%, 23.59%, and 8.75%, respectively (Fig. 3 a), which confirmed the composition of the N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3b, the high-resolution C1s spectrum showed two peaks at 284.7 eV and 286.0 eV, which can be attributed to C–C / C=C and C–OH / C–N [43]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) exhibited pyrrole (400.0 eV) and graphite (401.3 eV) [44], and the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) has three peaks at 531.0, 531.6 and 533.0 eV [45], which can be attributed to C–O, C=O, and C–OH / C–O–C. All the above results show that N and S were successfully doped in the N,S-CDs.

The UV–vis spectra of N,S-CDs showed two peaks at 280 nm, attributed to the π–π * transition of C = C, while multiple continuous absorption peaks between 500 and 600 nm may be attributed to the n–π * transition from C=O and C=N [46], as shown in Fig. 4 a. Under excitation at 530 nm, the N,S-CDs exhibited two bright red fluorescence peaks at 595 nm and 645 nm, which were further determined by CIE color coordinates (0.6264, 0.3731) (Fig. 4b). As shown in Fig. 4c, the emission wavelength of N,S-CDs does not change for different excitation wavelengths, indicating that the size and surface state of N,S-CDs are relatively uniform. The change in fluorescence intensity indicates that probe N,S-CDs possess excitation independence [47]. Furthermore, N,S-CDs exhibited a quantum efficiency of 20.81% using quinine sulfate as a reference (Fig. 4d), which is because the surface of N,S-CDs is rich in Coluo and pyrrole N.

NaCl solution was used to study the salt stability of N,S-CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 a; when NaCl solutions of concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 mol/L were added, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs showed little change. The probe N,S-CDs showed stable fluorescence even after irradiation under an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) for 1 h (Fig. 5b). In addition, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs was maintained even after 30 days (Fig. S3). Then we further explore the effects of PH, temperature and relative humidity on N,S-CDs. The results show that N,S-CDs has better fluorescence under neutral and weakly acidic conditions, which is in line with the practical application (Fig. 5c). When the temperature is higher than 30 ℃, the temperature will have a certain effect on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs (Fig. S4), but the effect is reversible when the temperature decreases. The relative humidity has almost no effect on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs (Fig. 5d). Excellent selectivity is required for probe applications in complex biological systems. Herein, some interferants, such as anions (OCl-, SO3 2-, F-, Br-, I-, HSO4 -, S2 -, NO2 –, CH3COO–, CO3 2–, NO3 –, and pyrophosphate (PPi), were used to evaluate the selectivity of N,S-CDs. The results revealed that at a consistent concentration, only PPi significantly enhanced the fluorescence of N,S-CDs (Fig. S5a). In addition, various amino acids, including cysteine, glutathione, homocysteine, phenylalanine, histidine, valine, methionine, lysine, tryptophan, tyrosine, threonine, and the tripeptide glutathione, were used to evaluate the immunity of the N,S-CDs probe. As expected, none of these amino acids caused any significant change in the fluorescence (Fig. S5b). These results showed that the N,S-CD probe has photostability and excellent selectivity, and thus can be used to detect PPi in complex environments.

Fluorescence titration experiments were further used to quantify the relationship between the PPi concentration and fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. With the addition of PPi at different concentrations ranging from 0 to 2.0 μM, both channels (595 and 645 nm) showed gradually enhanced fluorescence (Fig. S6), and solution color changed from weak pink fluorescence to bright red, which can be observed with the naked eye. At 595 nm, as the concentration of PPi added increased from 0 to 2.0 μM, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs gradually increased and a good linear relationship was obtained, as expressed by the equation Y = 4486.98 × + 2250.13 (R2 = 0.9884), while the concentration of PPi varied from 0 to 1.0 μM (Fig. 6 a). According to IUPAC regulations (LOD = 3 σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution, and K is the slope of the linear regression). The LOD was calculated to be 0.15 nM. At 645 nm, when the concentration of the added PPi ranged from 0 to 2.0 μM, a similar fluorescence phenomenon was observed with a gradual decrease in the observed increase over time. The linear equation Y = 1532.74 × + 1848.83 (R2 = 0.9682) was used to express the relationship between the concentration of PPi (0–1.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs. According to this equation, an LOD of 0.45 nM was calculated (Fig. 6b). The LOD values of both the 595 nm and 645 nm channels are much less than the national standard GB2760 for the use of additive PPi (≤5g/kg). In addition, the ratio relationship of the fluorescence enhancement in the two channels was further researched. As shown in Fig. 6c, the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio I595 nm/I645 nm and the PPi concentration (0–2.0 μM) was expressed as the logarithmic equation Y = 0.2441 ln (x) + 1.8979 (R2 = 0.9410), LOD is 0.22 nM. Fluorescence titration experiments indicated that N,S-CDs can be used as dual-channel fluorescent ratio probes for the detection of PPi.

First of all, the fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CDs with and without PPi were tested. The fluorescence lifetimes of N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs + PPi are 1.47 ns and 2.11 ns (Fig. S7a and S7b), respectively. The results are consistent with the experimental phenomena, which proves that PPi can enhance the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. According to the structural characterization described above, a large number of hydroxyl (–OH) and carbonyl groups (C = O) were present on the surface of the N,S-CDs. The protons on the hydroxyl groups can be transferred to the carbonyl groups, which causes non-radiative transitions and results in quenching of the fluorescence of N,S-CDs. As a proton donor and acceptor, PPi can deprotonate the hydroxyl group of N,S-CDs via formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds [48], thereby inhibiting excited-state proton transfer (ESPT) and activating the fluorescence of N,S-CDs (Fig. 7 ).

To further verify this mechanism, the effects of oxidants and reducers on the fluorescence of N,S-CDs were evaluated (Fig. S8a). N,S-CDs showed fluorescence quenching after treatment with KMnO4. Although –OH on the surface of N,S-CDs was oxidized to prevent the ESPT process, a significant increase in the amount of C=O on the surface of N,S-CDs produced many defects and inhibited fluorescence emission. As expected, N,S-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence after treatment with KBH4 because the C = O of N,S-CDs was reduced to –OH and blocked the ESPT process. These results indicated that the weak fluorescence of N,S-CDs was attributed to the ESPT process between the surface –OH and C = O groups [49]. In addition, we investigated the responsiveness of N,S-CDs pre-treated with KMnO4 and KBH4 to PPi. As shown in Fig. S8b, the fluorescence of N,S-CDs pre-treated with KMnO4 increased slightly after the addition of PPi, which may have been caused by the incomplete oxidation of –OH. N,S-CDs pre-treated with KBH4 did not respond to PPi (Fig. S8c) because the C=O on the surface of the N,S-CDs was reduced to –OH, and the ESPT process was blocked.

SEM images (Fig. S9a) were used to examine the morphology of the N,S-CDs before and after the addition of PPi, the N,S-CDs showed dispersed nanoparticles before the addition of PPi, and obvious aggregation was observed after the addition of PPi (Fig. S9b). This proved that PPi formed hydrogen bonds with N,S-CDs, causing aggregation. The aggregation of N,S-CDs forms a structure with higher rigidity, which inhibits non-radiative electron transitions, resulting in intense fluorescence [50].

Based on the results obtained from the fluorescence experiment, we further verified the detection effect of N,S-CDs in real samples. As a food additive, PPi is involved in many aspects of life. Tap water and milk were selected as test objects. Samples with different PPi contents were prepared by the standard addition method, and the PPi content was quantitatively determined by fluorescence spectroscopy using N,S-CDs. Table 1 shows that the recovery of PPi in the spiked samples, detected using N,S-CDs, was between 88.9% and 107.7%, indicating that N,S-CDs can be used for actual detection.

Owing to the excellent optical properties of N,S-CDs, imaging of N,S-CDs in living cells was further studied by confocal microscopy. First, the cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs on HeLa cells was assessed using the MTT assay, and the results are presented in Fig. S10. HeLa cells cultured with N,S-CDs for 24 h increased the concentration of N,S-CDs to 100 μg/mL, and the survival rate was maintained at more than 92%, indicating that the cytotoxicity of N,S-CDs was very low and suitable for live cell imaging. When N,S-CDs and PPi were incubated with HeLa cells, it was found that with only N,S-CDs (10 μg/mL), weak red fluorescence was collected in the red channel, and with the PPi (0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 μM) the additional fluorescence gradually increased (Fig. 8 ). The experimental results showed that N,S-CDs can be used for the imaging of living cells.

Inspired by the results of cell imaging, we continued to explore the applicability of N,S-CDs in vivo. Zebrafish are typical vertebrate animals and were therefore selected as the research object. As shown in Fig. 9 , N,S-CDs (10 μg/mL) for 1 h exhibited weak red fluorescence under a confocal microscope. In contrast, when different concentrations of PPi were added (0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 μM), a gradual enhancement of red fluorescence was observed, which is consistent with the results of previous cell experiments. These results demonstrated that N,S-CDs could successfully enter zebrafish and detect PPi using fluorescence signals.

Briefly, a metal-free auxiliary pyrophosphate (PPi) probe with dual-channel detection was constructed based on near-infrared nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs). The synthesized N,S-CDs showed excellent selectivity to PPi and revealed good linear relationship in the range from 0 to 1 mΜ, with an ultra-low detection limit of 0.22 nM. N,S-CDs showed excellent practical application, and the recovery rates of PPi in tap water and milk were 88.9% and 107.7%, respectively, the ideal results in cells and zebrafish experiments were also obtained. The development of such metal-free auxiliary PPi probes is in line with the development direction of probes, which can provide an accurate warning of trace PPi in both the environment and organisms and is beneficial to the improvement of the environment and the early diagnosis of related diseases.
Declarations
Studies on Zebrafish were conducted with permission from the Ethics Committee, Qilu Health Science Center, and Nanjing Normal University.

Liying Jin: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Visualization. Chuanfeng Zhao: Investigation. Xiaosong Wang: Investigation. Qian Zhang: Formal analysis. Yuliang Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jian Shen: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122580.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
